content.problemStatement:
Chef is stuck at the following problem. Help him solve it!

Chef has a sequence of integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​. He should find the number of pairs (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that 1≤i<j≤N1 \le i \lt j \le N1≤i<j≤N and the bitwise XOR of AiA_iAi​ and AjA_jAj​ can be written as a sum of two (not necessarily different) prime numbers with the same parity (both odd or both even).
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains a single integer N. The second line contains N space-separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the number of valid pairs.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The three valid pairs are (1,2), (1,3) and (2,3). For example, A1 ⊕ A2 = 2 ⊕ 4 = 6 = 3+3.
The only valid pair is (1,2). A1 ⊕ A2 = 1 ⊕ 2 = 3 = 2+1.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we first need to generate all prime numbers up to 10^6 using the Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm. Then, for each pair of numbers in the input array, we calculate their bitwise XOR. We check if the XOR can be expressed as a sum of two prime numbers with the same parity (both odd or both even) by iterating over the generated prime numbers. If such a pair of primes is found, we increment the count of valid pairs.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2 * sqrt(10^6)) — The time complexity is dominated by the iteration over all pairs of numbers in the input array and the iteration over the prime numbers to check if the XOR can be expressed as a sum of two primes.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(10^6) — The space complexity is dominated by the storage of the prime numbers generated using the Sieve of Eratosthenes algorithm.
